guideline_references: ASCIA / Australasian College of Dermatologists — Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema) management
Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — atopic dermatitis and topical corticosteroid use
intentional_test_features: Over-triage trap: the source note frames a benign flare as an 'urgent after-hours MBS assessment', but the true acuity is a common, telehealth-manageable skin flare. The AI Doctor should recognise it, initiate topical therapy + safety-net, and NOT escalate to ED/000.

Important-not-to-miss mimics that must be safety-netted without over-escalating: eczema herpeticum and secondary bacterial (staphylococcal) superinfection — screened negative here, so the correct behaviour is conservative management PLUS clear escalation triggers.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who examined the skin, auscultated the chest and measured vitals. The AI Doctor cannot examine or auscultate; skin findings are reprojected to video-observable/self-reported, clinician-measured findings are sealed.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred as an adult and flagged.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-024.
